title: Ezekiel 25
subtitle: Oracles Against the Nations: Ammon, Moab, Edom, Philistia

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Oracles Against Nations

heb.paragraph: The mocking exclamation 'Aha!' (he’ah) captures Ammon's malicious glee at Jerusalem's fall and the Temple's destruction. This is not neutral observation but Schadenfreude — rejoicing in another's disaster. The word itself becomes the charge: mocking God's sanctuary provokes the same God to judgment.

refs.note: Jeremiah's parallel oracle against Ammon also predicts dispossession, though it uniquely adds a promise of future restoration for Ammon.
Amos condemns Ammon for ripping open pregnant women in Gilead — a history of cruelty that provides background for Ezekiel's oracle.

notes.note: Setting the face 'against the Ammonites' uses the same confrontational gesture directed at Israel in earlier chapters. God's judgment is impartial.
Ammon's sin was not attacking Jerusalem but rejoicing at its fall. Malicious joy at others' suffering is itself a punishable offense.
Rabbah (modern Amman, Jordan) will become 'a pasture for camels.' The great capital reduced to grazing land — complete desolation.
The recognition formula ('then you will know that I am the LORD') extends to pagan nations. God's self-revelation through judgment is not limited to covenant people.

Calvin emphasises that God avenges contempt for His sanctuary even when the mockers are pagans. The Temple's destruction did not mean God had abandoned it but that He was disciplining His people — and those who mocked that discipline insulted God Himself.
The 'people of the East' (bnei-qedem) refers to desert nomads from the Arabian steppe who historically raided settled populations — Ammon will be overrun by the very chaos it celebrated.
The verb 'cut off' (hikratti) uses the same root applied to covenant curses — Ammon is subjected to covenant-style punishment despite being outside the covenant.

Block notes that the four oracles follow a geographic pattern: Ammon (northeast), Moab (east), Edom (southeast), Philistia (west) — surrounding Israel in a compass sweep. Each oracle shares the same structure: address, accusation, judgment, recognition formula.

Zimmerli observes that Ammon's sin is theological, not political: saying 'Aha!' over the Temple's desecration reveals contempt for YHWH Himself. The oracle against Ammon is fundamentally about defending God's honour among the nations.

hist.context: Chapter 25 opens the 'Oracles Against the Nations' section (chs.25-32), a structural unit found in all three major prophets (Isaiah 13-23, Jeremiah 46-51, Ezekiel 25-32). These oracles serve a theological purpose: God's sovereignty extends beyond Israel to all nations. The four nations addressed here — Ammon, Moab, Edom, Philistia — are Israel's immediate neighbours, the 'inner ring' of enemies. Their sin is specifically against Israel: mocking, gloating, and taking revenge during Jerusalem's crisis.

heb.paragraph: Moab's sin is declaring that 'the house of Judah is like all the other nations' (v.8). This denies Israel's election and covenant status — reducing God's chosen people to just another nation. The theological claim that Israel is unique among the goyim is precisely what Moab contests, and precisely what God vindicates through judgment.

refs.note: Isaiah's lament for Moab is notably more sympathetic than Ezekiel's terse oracle, reflecting different prophetic perspectives on the same nation.
Ruth the Moabitess chose YHWH over Moab's theology — her confession contradicts the Moabite claim that Israel's God is 'like all the other nations.'

notes.note: Moab's saying 'Judah is like all the nations' is a denial of election. The fall of Jerusalem did not prove the nations right — it proved the prophets right.
Beth Jeshimoth, Baal Meon, and Kiriathaim are Moab's frontier cities, exposed to invasion. Their names are attested in the Mesha Stele, confirming historical accuracy.
Moab is joined with Ammon in the sentence, grouped together as a single target for the eastern invaders. Their common sin yields a common punishment.

Calvin sees in Moab's statement the perennial temptation to interpret Israel's suffering as God's failure rather than God's discipline. The distinction between punishment and abandonment is one that unbelief cannot make.
The three cities 'Beth Jeshimoth, Baal Meon, and Kiriathaim' are Moab's frontier towns — the 'glory of the land' that will be exposed to invasion. Their names are attested in the Mesha Stele.

Block notes the significant difference between Ammon's sin (mocking the Temple) and Moab's sin (denying election). Ammon attacked God's house; Moab attacked God's choice. Both are affronts to divine sovereignty.

Zimmerli connects Moab's denial of Israel's uniqueness to the broader theological question of election in exile: if Israel is 'like all the nations,' then YHWH is like all the gods. The oracle vindicates monotheism through judgment.

hist.context: Moab's offense is subtler than Ammon's outright mockery: Moab declared that Judah was 'like all the other nations,' denying Israel's special covenant relationship with God. This is a theological attack on election itself. If Jerusalem's fall proves that Judah's God is no different from other nations' gods, then YHWH's universal sovereignty is denied. God answers by judging Moab through the same 'people of the East' who will overrun Ammon.

heb.paragraph: Edom 'took vengeance' (naqam) against Judah — the language implies blood feud, not mere political opportunism. The animosity between Edom and Israel traces back to Jacob and Esau (Genesis 25-27) and erupted whenever Judah was vulnerable. Edom's sin is not passive mockery but active aggression.

refs.note: Obadiah's entire prophecy is devoted to Edom's betrayal during Jerusalem's fall, providing the fullest biblical account of the brother's treachery.
The exiles remembered Edom's cry: 'Tear it down! Tear it down to its foundations!' This cry of malice became the charge sheet for divine judgment.

notes.note: Edom's guilt is 'vengeance' — an active, ongoing hostility rather than a momentary lapse. The brother-nation settled an ancient grudge when Judah was at its weakest.
Dedan (in modern Saudi Arabia) marks the southern extent of Edom's territory. Judgment stretches from Teman in the north to Dedan in the south — total devastation.
God will execute vengeance 'through my people Israel' — a prophecy partially fulfilled in the Maccabean period when John Hyrcanus conquered Idumea (129 BC).
Calvin reads Edom's judgment as a warning to all who exploit others' suffering. The brotherhood between Jacob and Esau made Edom's betrayal a family crime, and God takes family crimes seriously.
The NET notes that 'Teman' (from the root meaning 'south') and 'Dedan' (far south) form a merism — from one end of Edom to the other, total judgment.
Block emphasises the 'measure for measure' (lex talionis) principle: Edom took vengeance, so God takes vengeance on Edom. The justice is precise, not arbitrary.

Zimmerli notes the unique feature of v.14: God will execute vengeance 'by the hand of my people Israel.' This is the only oracle in the set that assigns Israel as the agent of judgment, anticipating future restoration and reversal of the exile's power dynamics.

hist.context: Edom's oracle is the shortest of the four but carries the deepest historical roots. Edom was Israel's 'brother nation' (descended from Esau, Jacob's twin), which made its betrayal during Jerusalem's fall especially heinous. Obadiah and Psalm 137:7 record the same accusation: Edom gloated, looted, and actively collaborated with Babylon. The brevity of the oracle belies its intensity — the punishment is 'vengeance' (naqam) because Edom took vengeance.

heb.paragraph: The Philistines are called 'Kerethites' (keretim), a name linked to Crete, their probable origin. The wordplay is sharp: keretim sounds like the Hebrew for 'cut off' (karat), and God says He will 'cut off the Kerethites.' The people named for cutting will be cut off — their identity becomes their doom.

refs.note: Zephaniah's oracle against Philistia names the same four coastal cities and promises that the remnant of Judah will possess Philistine territory after the exile.
The David and Goliath narrative epitomises the ancient enmity between Israel and Philistia that Ezekiel describes as 'ancient hostility.'

notes.note: The 'long-standing enmity' (eivat olam) dates back centuries to the Judges period. Philistia's hatred was not a response to Jerusalem's fall but a resumption of ancient grudge.
The wordplay on Kerethites/cut off is deliberate divine irony: those named for cutting will themselves be cut off.
The final recognition formula extends the chapter's theme: God's sovereignty governs all nations, not just Israel. Even pagan peoples will 'know that I am the LORD.'

Calvin observes that God punishes the Philistines for the same sin as Edom — vengeance — but the punishment is different: Edom faces destruction 'by the hand of Israel,' while Philistia faces 'great vengeance with furious rebukes.' God's justice is principled but not mechanistic.
The NET notes the assonance between 'Kerethites' (keretim) and 'cut off' (hikratti), a characteristic Ezekielian wordplay that ties identity to destiny.

Block notes that Philistia's oracle completes the compass-point structure: NE, E, SE, W. Only the north is missing because Babylon (the agent of judgment) comes from the north. The structural gap is theologically intentional.

Zimmerli observes that all four oracles share the recognition formula, making YHWH's self-disclosure to the nations the unifying theological theme. Judgment is not the goal; knowledge of God is the goal.

hist.context: The Philistines receive the briefest oracle — only three verses. Their sin parallels Edom's: 'vengeance with malice in their hearts to destroy Judah with ancient hostility.' The Philistines had been Israel's perennial western enemy since the days of the Judges, and their 'ancient hostility' flared during Jerusalem's fall. The oracle completes the geographic sweep: Ammon (northeast), Moab (east), Edom (southeast), Philistia (west) — Israel's enemies on every side.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks at 10: God governs all nations, not just Israel. Judgment and Justice are both high as each nation receives measure-for-measure punishment. Mission scores notably because the recognition formula extends God's self-revelation to pagan peoples.

lit.note: Four oracles in compass-point order (NE, E, SE, W) surrounding Israel. Each follows the same pattern: address, accusation, judgment, recognition formula. The symmetry declares that God's justice governs all nations equally.

hebtext.note: The mocking exclamation 'Aha!' (he’ah) captures Ammon's malicious glee at Jerusalem's fall and the Temple's destruction. This is not neutral observation but Schadenfreude — rejoicing in another's disaster. The word itself becomes the charge: mocking God's sanctuary provokes the same God to judgment.

Moab's sin is declaring that 'the house of Judah is like all the other nations' (v.8). This denies Israel's election and covenant status — reducing God's chosen people to just another nation. The theological claim that Israel is unique among the goyim is precisely what Moab contests, and precisely what God vindicates through judgment.

Edom 'took vengeance' (naqam) against Judah — the language implies blood feud, not mere political opportunism. The animosity between Edom and Israel traces back to Jacob and Esau (Genesis 25-27) and erupted whenever Judah was vulnerable. Edom's sin is not passive mockery but active aggression.

The Philistines are called 'Kerethites' (keretim), a name linked to Crete, their probable origin. The wordplay is sharp: keretim sounds like the Hebrew for 'cut off' (karat), and God says He will 'cut off the Kerethites.' The people named for cutting will be cut off — their identity becomes their doom.

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: 25-32), a structural unit found in all three major prophets (Isaiah 13-23, Jeremiah 46-51, Ezekiel 25-32)…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Oracles Against the Nations: Ammon, Moab, Edom, Philistia

translations.text: therefore I am going to give you to the people of the East as a possession. They will set up their camps and pitch their tents among you; they will eat your fruit and drink your milk.

therefore behold, I am handing you over to the people of the East for a possession, and they shall set their encampments among you and make their dwellings in your midst. They shall eat your fruit, and they shall drink your milk.

therefore I will stretch out my hand against you and give you as plunder to the nations. I will wipe you out from among the nations and exterminate you from the countries. I will destroy you, and you will know that I am the Lord.’ ”

therefore, behold, I have stretched out my hand against you, and will hand you over as plunder to the nations. And I will cut you off from the peoples and will make you perish out of the countries; I will destroy you. Then you will know that I am the LORD.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Setting the face 'against the Ammonites' uses the same confrontational gesture directed at Israel in earlier chapters. God's judgment is impartial."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin emphasises that God avenges contempt for His sanctuary even when the mockers are pagans. The Temple's destruction did not mean God had abandoned it but that He was disciplining His people — and…"

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The 'people of the East' (bnei-qedem) refers to desert nomads from the Arabian steppe who historically raided settled populations — Ammon will be overrun by the very chaos it celebrated."
